subject: Cardiology
title: Chronic coronary disease
preambleMarkdown:
# Chronic coronary disease

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Chronic coronary disease is part of the coronary artery disease spectrum in which atherosclerotic plaque, thrombosis, vasomotor dysfunction, or supply-demand imbalance reduces myocardial perfusion. Exam questions emphasize risk recognition, ECG and biomarker interpretation, initial stabilization, prevention, and choosing medical therapy, PCI, CABG, or rehabilitation in the correct sequence.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Smoking, diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, chronic kidney disease, and family history are major risk factors.
- ECG findings must be interpreted with symptoms, prior tracings, and serial biomarkers.
- Troponin elevation indicates myocardial injury and needs clinical classification.
- Lifestyle change and risk-factor control are central, not optional.
- Revascularization treats selected anatomy or unstable syndromes; it does not replace medical prevention.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Chronic coronary disease refers to clinically important myocardial ischemia or coronary atherosclerotic disease, ranging from stable plaque burden to acute plaque disruption depending on the specific syndrome.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stable chronic coronary disease.
- Acute coronary syndrome and myocardial infarction.
- Obstructive versus nonobstructive coronary disease.
- Native-vessel disease versus post-revascularization disease.
- Primary prevention, secondary prevention, and post-event rehabilitation states.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Coronary disease becomes more common with age and clusters with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, chronic kidney disease, family history, inflammatory disease, and prior vascular disease. Exams often test risk stratification, secondary prevention, and atypical presentations in older adults, women, diabetes, and kidney disease.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - The clinically important anatomy is the coronary circulation: left main, LAD, circumflex, RCA, dominance pattern, septal perforators, marginal branches, posterior descending artery, and papillary muscle blood supply. Anatomy explains ECG localization, wall-motion abnormalities, mechanical complications, and PCI or CABG planning.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Myocardial oxygen balance depends on coronary blood flow, diastolic perfusion time, oxygen content, heart rate, afterload, wall stress, and contractility. Ischemia develops when supply cannot meet demand, especially during plaque rupture, fixed stenosis, vasospasm, anemia, hypoxemia, tachyarrhythmia, or shock.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Atherosclerotic plaque narrows coronary arteries and can rupture or erode, exposing thrombogenic material and forming platelet-rich thrombus. Downstream ischemia causes electrical instability, contractile dysfunction, biomarker release when necrosis occurs, and remodeling after injury.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Causes include obstructive atherosclerosis, acute plaque rupture, supply-demand mismatch, vasospasm, coronary embolism, spontaneous coronary artery dissection, microvascular dysfunction, anemia, hypoxemia, and tachyarrhythmia. The exact cause determines whether the event is type 1 MI, type 2 MI, nonischemic injury, or chronic coronary disease.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess smoking, diabetes, hypertension, LDL cholesterol, chronic kidney disease, family history of premature ASCVD, obesity, sedentary behavior, inflammatory disease, sleep apnea, prior preeclampsia, early menopause, and prior coronary or vascular disease.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Chest pressure or tightness, dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea, fatigue, syncope, or reduced exercise tolerance.
- Atypical presentations occur in older adults, women, diabetes, kidney disease, and postoperative patients.
- Complications may include arrhythmia, heart failure, cardiogenic shock, or sudden death.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ongoing chest pain with ischemic ECG changes.
- Hypotension, pulmonary edema, syncope, or malignant arrhythmia.
- New murmur or mechanical complication after infarction.
- Chest pain suggesting aortic dissection before antithrombotics.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Important alternatives include aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism, pericarditis, myocarditis, pneumothorax, pneumonia, esophageal spasm, reflux, biliary disease, panic symptoms, costochondritis, and nonischemic myocardial injury.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then obtain ECG and focused history.
- Use serial troponins when ACS is possible.
- Use risk scores, stress imaging, coronary CTA, or invasive angiography according to acuity and pretest probability.
- Evaluate lipids, diabetes, kidney function, anemia, and other risk modifiers.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use ECG, serial troponins, renal function, electrolytes, CBC, lipid and diabetes assessment, chest imaging when indicated, echocardiography for wall motion or complications, stress testing or coronary CTA for selected stable patients, and invasive angiography for high-risk or occlusion syndromes.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Stabilize unstable patients and activate emergency pathways when ACS is suspected.
- Use antiplatelet, anticoagulant, anti-ischemic, lipid-lowering, and blood-pressure therapies according to syndrome and contraindications.
- Select PCI or CABG based on anatomy, acuity, diabetes, left main or multivessel disease, surgical risk, and patient goals.
- Refer to cardiac rehabilitation and arrange long-term secondary prevention.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ACS is diagnosed by symptoms, ECG, and serial troponins.
- Do not call every troponin elevation type 1 MI.
- Secondary prevention includes smoking cessation, lipid lowering, antiplatelet therapy when indicated, blood-pressure control, diabetes care, and rehabilitation.
- CABG is favored in selected left main, complex multivessel, or diabetes-associated disease.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: lib.md import start -->
Source: Core clinical source notes. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Stable Angina Pectoris

_Source lines: 93-480._

```text
Stable Angina Pectoris
A. General Characteristics
1. Stable angina pectoris is due to fixed atherosclerotic lesions that
narrow the major coronary arteries. Coronary ischemia is due to an
imbalance between blood supply and oxygen demand, leading to
inadequate perfusion. Stable angina occurs when oxygen demand
exceeds available blood supply. Also called chronic coronary
syndrome or stable ischemic heart disease
2. Major risk factors
a. Diabetes mellitus (DM)-worst risk factor
b. Hyperlipidemia-elevated low-density lipoprotein (LDL)
c. Hypertension (HTN)-most common risk factor
d. Cigarette smoking
e. Age (men >45 years; women >55 years)
f. Family history of premature CAD or MI in first-degree relative:
Men <55 years; women <65 years
g. Low levels of high-density lipoprotein (HDL)
3. Less common risk factors include end-stage renal disease (ESRD) on
hemodialysis, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection,
history of mediastinal radiation. Minor risk factors include obesity,
sedentary lifestyle (lack of physical activity), stress, excess alcohol

use

## 4. Prognostic indicators of CAD

a. Left ventricular function (ejection fraction [EF])
- Normal >50%
- If <50%, associated with increased mortality
d. Vessel(s) involved (severity/extent of ischemia)
- Left main coronary artery-poor prognosis because it
supplies approximately two-thirds of the heart
- Two- or three-vessel CAD-worse prognosis
Quick HIT
CAD can have the following clinical presentations:
- Asymptomatic
- Stable angina pectoris
- Unstable angina
- MI-either NSTEMI or STEMI
- Sudden cardiac death
Quick HIT
Typical Anginal Chest Pain
- Substernal
- Worse with exertion
- Better with rest or nitroglycerin
B. Clinical Features
1. Chest pain or substernal pressure sensation
a. Lasts less than 10 to 15 minutes (usually 1 to 5 minutes)
b. Frightening chest discomfort, usually described as heaviness,
pressure, squeezing, tightness; rarely described as sharp or
stabbing pain
c. Pain is often gradual in onset
2. Brought on by factors that increase myocardial oxygen demand,
such as exertion, stress, or drugs (i.e., cocaine or amphetamines)
3. Relieved with rest or nitroglycerin
4. Factors that are less consistent with ischemic pain (but do not rule
out ischemia): Pain that changes with breathing or body position,
or chest wall tenderness
Quick HIT

Stress testing is used in the following situations:
- To confirm diagnosis of angina
- To evaluate response to therapy in patients with documented
CAD
- To identify patients with CAD who may have a high risk of acute
coronary events
C. Diagnosis
1. Note that physical examination in most patients with CAD is
normal. Resting ECG
a. Usually normal in patients with stable angina
b. Q waves are consistent with a prior MI
2. Stress test-useful for patients with an intermediate pretest
probability of CAD based upon age, gender, and symptoms.

a. General principles: Exercise stress testing is preferred if the
patient can exercise, whereas pharmacologic stress testing is
preferred when they cannot
b. Stress ECG
- Highest sensitivity if patients have normal resting ECG, such
that changes can be noted. Patients with baseline ECG
abnormalities such as left bundle branch block (LBBB), pre-
existing ST-segment changes, or left ventricular hypertrophy
(LVH), should NOT undergo stress ECG testing.

- Test involves recording ECG before, during, and after
exercise on a treadmill.

- 75% sensitive if patients are able to exercise suficiently to
increase heart rate to 85% of maximum predicted value for
age. A person's maximum heart rate is calculated by
subtracting age from 220 (220 - age).

- Exercise-induced ischemia results in subendocardial
ischemia, producing ST-segment depression. So the detection
of ischemia on a stress ECG test is based on the presence of
ST-segment depression.

- Other positive findings include onset of heart failure or
ventricular arrhythmia during exercise or hypotension.

- Patients with a positive stress test result should undergo
coronary angiography.

i. Stress echocardiography
- Performed before and immediately after exercise. Exercise-
induced ischemia is evidenced by wall motion abnormalities
(e.g., akinesis or hypokinesis) not present at rest. This study
is less reliable in patients with existing wall motion
abnormalities or lowered EF.

- Useful to assess LV size and function, can diagnose valvular
disease, and can be used to identify CAD in the presence of
pre-existing ECG abnormalities (see Clinical Pearl 1-1).

l. Information gained from a stress test can be enhanced by stress
myocardial perfusion imaging after IV administration of a
radioisotope such as thallium 201 during exercise.

- Viable myocardial cells extract the radioisotope from the
blood. No radioisotope uptake means no blood flow to an
area of the myocardium.

- It is important to determine whether the ischemia is reversible,
that is, whether areas of hypoperfusion are perfused over
time as blood flow eventually equalizes. Areas of reversible
ischemia may be rescued with percutaneous coronary
intervention (PCI) or coronary artery bypass graft (CABG).

Irreversible ischemia, however, indicates infarcted tissue
that cannot be salvaged.

- Perfusion imaging increases the sensitivity and specificity of
exercise stress tests, but is also more expensive and subjects
the patient to radiation.

Quick HIT
Exercise stress ECG is an ideal initial test if able to exercise and
have a normal resting ECG.

Quick HIT
A stress test is generally considered positive if the patient develops
any of the following during exercise: ST-segment depression, chest
pain, hypotension, or significant arrhythmias.

1. If the patient cannot exercise, perform a pharmacologic stress test.

a. IV adenosine, dipyridamole, or dobutamine can be used. The
cardiac stress induced by these agents takes the place of
exercise. This can be combined with an ECG, an
echocardiogram, or nuclear perfusion imaging.

b. IV adenosine and dipyridamole cause generalized coronary
vasodilation. Since diseased coronary arteries are already
maximally dilated at rest to increase blood flow, they receive
relatively less blood flow when the entire coronary system is
pharmacologically vasodilated. These agents are used for
perfusion imaging.

c. Dobutamine increases myocardial oxygen demand by increasing
heart rate, blood pressure, and cardiac contractility. This is
typically used in stress echo studies.

CLINICAL PEARL 1-1
Types of Stress Tests
Test 	Method of Detecting
Ischemia
Exercise ECG
Exercise or dobutamine
echocardiogram
Exercise or dipyridamole perfusion
study (thallium/technetium)
ST-segment depression
Wall motion abnormalities
Decreased uptake of the nuclear
isotope during exercise
CLINICAL PEARL 1-2
Cardiac Catheterization
1. Most accurate method of determining a specific cardiac diagnosis.

2. Provides information on hemodynamics, intracardiac pressure
measurements, cardiac output, oxygen saturation, etc.

3. Coronary angiography (see below) is almost always performed as well for
visualization of coronary arteries.

4. There are many indications for cardiac catheterization (generally
performed when revascularization or other surgical intervention is being
considered):
- After a positive stress test.

- Acute MI with intent of performing angiogram and PCI.

- In a patient with angina in any of the following situations: When
noninvasive tests are nondiagnostic, angina that occurs despite
medical therapy, angina that occurs soon after MI, and any angina
that is a diagnostic dilemma.

- If a patient is severely symptomatic and urgent diagnosis and
management are necessary.

- For evaluation of valvular disease, and to determine the need for
surgical intervention.

Coronary Angiography
1. Most accurate method of identifying the presence and severity of CAD;

the standard test for delineating coronary anatomy.

2. Main purpose is to identify patients with severe coronary disease to
determine whether revascularization is needed. Revascularization with
PCI involving a balloon and/or a stent can be performed at the same time
as the diagnostic procedure.

3. Coronary stenosis >70% may be significant (i.e., it can produce angina).

1. Cardiac catheterization with coronary angiography (see Clinical
Pearl 1-2; Figure 1-1).

a. Coronary angiography-definitive test for CAD. Often
performed with concurrent PCI or for patients being considered
for revascularization with CABG.

b. This is the test of choice for patients with very high pretest
probability of CAD.

c. Contrast is injected into coronary vessels to visualize any
stenotic lesions. This defines the location and extent of coronary
disease.

d. If CAD is severe (e.g., left main or three-vessel disease, or two-
vessel disease with concurrent DM), refer patient for surgical
revascularization (CABG).

2. Other imaging studies to diagnose CAD include CT scan with
coronary artery calcium score, or a CT angiogram. These are
typically used in patients at intermediate risk who cannot exercise.

FIGURE
1-1 Coronary angiogram. Injection of the right coronary artery shows a
stenosis in the midportion of the vessel, indicated by the arrow.

(Courtesy of Pinak B. Shah, MD, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Boston, MA.)

D. Treatment
1. Risk factor modification
a. Smoking cessation cuts CAD risk in half by 1 year after quitting.

b. HTN-vigorous BP control reduces the risk of CAD, especially in
diabetic patients.

c. Hyperlipidemia-reduction in serum cholesterol with lifestyle
modifications and HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (statins)
reduce CAD risk. Again, goal LDL is <70 mg/dL in patients
with known CAD.

d. DM-type II diabetes is considered to be a cardiovascular heart
disease equivalent, and strict glycemic control should be
strongly emphasized.

e. Obesity-weight loss modifies other risk factors (diabetes, HTN,
and hyperlipidemia) and provides other health benefits.

f. Exercise is critical; it minimizes emotional stress, promotes
weight loss, and helps reduce other risk factors.

g. Diet: Reduce intake of saturated fat (<7% total calories) and
cholesterol (<200 mg/day).

Quick HIT
Standard of care for stable angina includes:
- Drugs that improve mortality: Aspirin, high-intensity statins (or in
some cases a PCSK9 inhibitor to lower LDL even further)
- Drugs that relieve angina: beta-blockers, nitrates, calcium channel
blockers, and ranolazine
Quick HIT
Side effects of beta-blockers:
- Erectile dysfunction in males
- Inability to increase HR in response to exercise
Side effects of nitrates:
- Headache
- Orthostatic hypotension
- Tolerance
- Syncope
1. Medical therapy
a. Aspirin
- Indicated in all patients with CAD
- Decreases morbidity-reduces risk of MI
d. Lipid-lowering therapy
- HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (statins) lower LDL and
reduce risk of CV death
- PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL even further than statins and
have been found to reduce CV events in patients on statin
therapy with LDL levels above 70
g. beta-Blockers-block sympathetic stimulation of the heart
- Reduce HR, BP, and contractility, thereby decreasing cardiac
work (i.e., beta-blockers lower myocardial oxygen
consumption)
- Have been shown to reduce the frequency of coronary
events
j. Nitrates-cause coronary vasodilation and systemic
venodilation

- Relieve angina; reduce preload myocardial oxygen demand
- May prevent angina when taken before exertion
- Effect on prognosis is unknown; main benefit is symptomatic
relief
- Can be administered orally, sublingually, transdermally,
intravenously, or in paste form. For chronic angina, oral or
transdermal patches are used. For acute coronary syndromes
(see below), either sublingual, paste, or IV forms are used
o. Calcium channel blockers (CCBs)
- Cause coronary vasodilation and afterload reduction, reduce
contractility
- Add to antianginal therapy when beta-blockers and nitrates are
not fully effective

## 2. Revascularization

a. Controversial for patients with stable CAD, as studies suggest no
improvement in mortality and MI (when compared to medical
therapy alone). Per guidelines, revascularization is indicated for
stable angina refractory to medical therapy for symptom
control.

b. Two methods-PCI and CABG-see Clinical Pearl 1-3.

3. Management decisions (general guidelines)-risk factor
modification and aspirin are indicated in all patients. Manage
patients according to overall risk
a. Mild disease (normal EF, mild angina, single-vessel disease)
- Nitrates (for symptoms and as prophylaxis) and a beta-blocker
are appropriate
- Consider CCBs if symptoms continue despite nitrates and beta-
blockers
d. Moderate disease (normal EF, moderate angina, two-vessel
disease)
- If the above regimen does not control symptoms, consider
coronary angiography to assess suitability for
revascularization (either PCI or CABG)
f. Severe disease (reduced EF, severe angina, and three-vessel/left
main or left anterior descending [LAD] disease)
- Coronary angiography and consider for CABG
- Avoid CCBs with reduced EF
Quick HIT
The COURAGE trial showed essentially no difference in all-cause
mortality and nonfatal MIs between patients with stable angina
treated with maximal medical therapy alone versus medical therapy

with PCI and bare metal stenting. Of note, most PCI is now done
with drug-eluting stents which are superior to bare metal stents with
regard to risk of stent thrombosis and recurrent MI.

CLINICAL PEARL 1-3
Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
- Consists of both coronary angioplasty with a balloon and stenting.

- Should be considered in patients with one-, two-, or three-vessel disease.

Even with three-vessel disease, mortality and freedom from MI have been
shown to be equivalent between PTCA with stenting and CABG. The only
drawback is the higher frequency of revascularization procedures in
patients who received a stent.

- Best if used for proximal lesions.

- Restenosis is a significant problem (up to 40% within first 6 months);
however, if there is no evidence of restenosis at 6 months, it usually does
not occur. New techniques and technologic improvements such as drug-
eluting stents are attempting to reduce this problem.
```

### Source Excerpt: Variant (Prinzmetal) Angina

_Source lines: 1116-1139._

```text
Variant (Prinzmetal) Angina
- Involves transient coronary vasospasm that usually is accompanied by
a fixed atherosclerotic lesion but can also occur in normal coronary
arteries.

- Episodes of angina occur at rest and are associated with ventricular
arrhythmias, some of which may be life threatening. The angina
classically occurs at night.

- Hallmark is transient ST-segment elevation (not depression) on ECG
during chest pain, which represents transmural ischemia.

- Coronary angiography is a definitive test-displays coronary
vasospasm when the patient is given IV ergonovine or acetylcholine
(to provoke vasoconstriction).

- Vasodilators-CCBs and nitrates have been proven to be helpful. Risk
factor modification including smoking cessation and lipid lowering is
also indicated where appropriate.

Quick HIT
Often, both systolic and diastolic dysfunctions are present
simultaneously.
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

sections.heading: References

- ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI. 2025 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2025-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-acute-coronary-syndromes
- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
